retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Insufficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A meta-analysis of 6 studies showed a 4 to 29% higher incidence of acute myocardial infarction in the spring daylight saving shift. [53] An increase in ischemic heart disease in males and females older than 60 years, as well as in road traffic accidents, has been reported in association with daylight saving time. [54] [55] The American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) published a position statement in 2020 supporting a year-round standard time based on current evidence. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Insufficiency -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A meta-analysis showed that short and long sleep duration is associated with an increased risk of all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease, coronary heart disease, and stroke, with the lowest risk noted at around 7 hours of sleep. A 1-hour reduction in sleep compared to 7 hours per day was associated with a 6% increase in all-cause mortality. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Somnambulism -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Somnambulism is a common arousal disorder. However, the epidemiology of this disorder is still ambiguous. The prevalence rate of sleepwalking is significantly higher in children than in adults. A systematic review and meta-analysis by Stallman and colleagues showed the estimated lifetime prevalence of sleepwalking is 6.9% without a significant difference in lifetime reports of sleepwalking between children and adults. The prevalence of sleepwalking within the past 12 months was reported to be significantly higher in children, 5.0% compared to 1.5% in adults. This is likely related to less slow-wave sleep during adulthood, leading to fewer prospects for sleepwalking. [11] Sleepwalking usually occurs in children but can continue into adulthood or appear de novo among the adult population. [11] [12] Studies suggest that relatively few people start sleepwalking as adults, and adult-onset somnambulism is usually associated with medications and neurodegenerative diseases. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: REM Rebound Effect -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: OSA leads to poor sleep quality in patients. The gold standard for diagnosing OSA is overnight PSG, and the most effective treatment is overnight CPAP. [20] A meta-analysis comparing OSA treatments shows no significant difference in outcomes between CPAP and bilevel-PAP. Nevertheless, treatment compliance remains a significant challenge for all OSA treatments, with the exception of surgical interventions. REM sleep rebound following initial OSA treatment is associated with improved sleep quality and elevated mood. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Obstructive Sleep Apnea ," for more information. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: REM Rebound Effect -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Adequate sleep is essential for physical health and overall well-being, though the exact prevalence of chronic sleep deficiency within the population remains to be accurately quantified. [11] Recent studies show that average nightly sleep duration has decreased by about 1 hour globally over the past century, with the most significant reductions occurring in highly industrialized areas such as Europe, North America, and Asia. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleeplessness and Circadian Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of the various circadian rhythm sleep disorders is unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Non-pharmacological
retrieved_docs.content: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) includes a set of psychological and behavioral techniques specific to treating insomnia. Studies report that CBT-I is the psychological treatment of choice, using individual or group therapy techniques and, recently, digital CBT-I formats. [54] [55] A meta-analysis of 61 randomized controlled trials, which included 11,571 participants and assessed different CBT-I delivery formats (individual, group, guided self-help, digital assisted, and unguided self-help ) with control conditions found that CBT-I not only significantly increases sleep parameters such as sleep efficiency and total sleep time but reduces sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, and insomnia severity. [54] CBT-I therapy is particularly important in groups of patients that may not tolerate pharmacological treatment, such as older patients, due to increased risk of side effects and addiction and tolerance to using Z drugs. [46] Other interventions are not proven to have a clinical effect if used alone. [56] These interventions include: Sleep restriction therapy (SRT): SRT limits the total time allowed in bed to increase the drive to sleep. Stimulus control therapy helps change sleep habits so that the patients don't have difficulty falling asleep. Patients should not go to bed until they are sleepy. Also, the bed should be used only for sleeping and not for watching television or reading books. Relaxation therapy: Relaxation techniques may be implemented before sleep. Meditation and breathing exercises are some of the relaxation techniques. Sleep hygiene: A set of education about lifestyle and environmental factors (e.g., light, noise, temperature) that may interfere with sleep. Sleep hygiene may include education about normal sleep, avoidance of substance use, regular exercise, bedroom environment, sleep and wake times, and avoidance of daytime naps. However, sleep hygiene education alone is less effective than CBT-I in individuals with poor sleep or insomnia. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Insufficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The AASM guidelines suggest using actigraphy to evaluate total sleep time in adults suspected of insufficient sleep syndrome. Compared to patient-reported sleep logs, actigraphy provides objective data that may be valuable in assessing poor sleep. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of sleep disorders differs based on the specific condition. A link between SDB, short sleep duration, and non-restorative sleep has recently been reported. [20] A recent study has linked poor sleep to other sleep disorders; for example, among a sample of patients with diabetes, 61% reported poor sleep, 47% of the participants screened positive for RLS, and 51% had an increased risk for OSA. [21] We will briefly list the prevalence of each major sleep disorder as classified by the ICSD-3.

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Insufficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The International Classification of Sleep Disorders or ICSD-3 criteria requires that several criteria be met to establish a diagnosis of insufficient sleep syndrome. [5] These include:

retrieved_docs.title: Melatonin -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Insomnia: Despite the lack of FDA approval, the American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) recognizes melatonin as the first-line pharmacological therapy for insomnia, emphasizing its crucial role in managing sleep-related concerns. This drug is relatively safe with a low risk of adverse effects. Research on the efficacy of melatonin supplementation for treating insomnia has shown varied results. However, a meta-analysis conducted by Ferracioli-Oda et al concluded that individuals who used melatonin experienced an average reduction in sleep onset latency by approximately 7 minutes, an increase in total sleep duration by approximately 8 minutes, and subjectively reported an improvement in the quality of their sleep, as compared to those who were given a placebo. Nevertheless, there was considerable variability in doses, study outcomes, and overall study quality. [3]

retrieved_docs.content: الدماغي غير المعدي, SMQ) and is grouped with broader cognitive impairments such as attention deficit, reduced concentration, and excessive early morning sleep, underscoring these features as characteristic of this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 157

The non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (SMQ) similarly features apathy (اللامبالاة), attention impairment, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Consequently, excessive daytime sleepiness serves as a linking symptom across arousal disturbances in both encephalopathic and delirium‑like SMQ categories, underscoring its clinical relevance for clinicians managing non‑infectious brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 220
retrieved_docs.content: encephalitis spectrum includes the two awakening disorders and is linked to motor and muscular hyperactivity and excessive daytime sleepiness. Likewise, “اللامبالاة” is a component of the delirium/brain‑damage category “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ),” which also comprises attention deficits, reduced concentration, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Collectively, these interrelated entities depict a heterogeneous network in which depressive, psychotic, substance‑use, encephalitic, and delirium‑related conditions share overlapping clinical features—such as reduced affect, hyperactivity, sleep‑wake disturbances, and cognitive impairments—underscoring the necessity for integrated assessment and personalized management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 196

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: transient and persistent sleep‑onset disorders are considered manifestations of non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ), which also presents with motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Apathy belongs to the delirium/non‑infectious brain pathology (SMQ) category and is linked to attention deficits, poor concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. The overlapping symptomatology of hyperactivity, excessive daytime sleepiness, and concentration impairment across depression, non‑infectious encephalitis, and delirium underscores the necessity for careful differential diagnosis and integrated, individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is described as a heterogeneous clinical entity that incorporates a wide spectrum of depressive subtypes, affective and cognitive symptoms, sleep‑wake disturbances, substance‑related contexts, and therapeutic interventions. The major depressive disorder subtypes—“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (single episode), and “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (single episode in full remission)—are grouped with additional forms such as “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ,” “اكتئاب تفاعلي” (reactive depression), “اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ” (endogenous depression), and “اكتئاب سوداوي” (melancholic depression). Emotional manifestations include emotional lability (“التقلقل الوجداني”), guilt (“الشعور بالذنب”), emotional poverty (“الفقر العاطفي”), indifference (“اللامبالاة”), heightened vigilance (“اليقظة”), and mood swings (“تقلب الحالة المزاجية”). Cognitive‑behavioral features encompass attention deficit,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: (SMQ)”) includes both transient and persistent sleep‑onset disorders and is linked to motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and primary daytime hypersomnia. Apathy “اللامبالاة” is connected to delirium/non‑infectious brain disorder (“الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”), which also presents with attention deficits, reduced concentration, and primary daytime hypersomnia. These interrelationships illustrate how personality problems, anhedonia, self‑destructive behaviors, substance‑use detoxification, withdrawal phenomena, diverse depressive subtypes, sleep disturbances, motor hyperactivity, and psychotic conditions intersect. Recognizing the full network of entities and their connections is essential for integrated assessment and comprehensive treatment planning in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are organized under the broader concept of الهَذَيان (delirium), and الهَذَيان in turn includes the specific category حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة (transient organic delirium cases). Both التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ) (non‑infectious encephalitis) and الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑infectious brain disorder) are SMQ groupings that list حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة as members. Within التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ), the following clinical features are identified as members: الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه (transient disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره (continuous disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), فرط النشاط الحركي (hyperkinetic activity), فرط النشاط العضلي (muscle hyperactivity), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness). In the related grouping الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ), the entities اللامبالاة (apathy), خلل في الانتباه (attention deficit), ضعف التركيز (poor concentration), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness) are listed as members. This hierarchical structure shows how delirium‑related behavioral and psychiatric manifestations are linked to specific transient organic delirium presentations and how those presentations are further categorized under distinct non‑infectious encephalitic and delirium brain‑disorder SMQ clusters, each characterized by particular sleep‑wake disturbances, motor abnormalities, and cognitive‑affective symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: (SMQ) extend the network to neurological disorders. Both conditions feature hypermotor and hypermuscular activity, early‑day hypersomnia, and disturbances of arousal regulation, specifically “الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه” (transient disorder of initiating or maintaining awakening) and “الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره” (persistent disorder of initiating or maintaining awakening). Habituation (“الاعتياد”) is also subsumed under the depression umbrella, reflecting shared dysregulation of circadian and arousal processes. The delirium/encephalopathy category adds indifference (“اللامبالاة”), attention deficits, and concentration weakness to the overlapping symptom profile. Collectively, these relationships portray depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) as a central, integrative syndrome that intersects with psychotic disorders, early‑onset developmental psychoses, and non‑infectious neuroinflammatory conditions, sharing common affective, cognitive,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
retrieved_docs.content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Schizophrenic mania (“hous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: excessive daytime sleepiness. Delirium/non‑inflammatory brain pathology includes apathy (اللامبالاة), attention disorder, concentration weakness, and excessive daytime sleepiness. The term ذُهان عضوي describes mental disorders with an organic or biological basis and subsumes a broader class of organic psychotic disorders. Within this class, هوس شَبَقِيّ is identified as a specific type of delusional mania that exhibits psychotic‑like features and is classified as an organic psychotic disorder. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how mental‑symptom domains intersect with organic psychotic conditions, how diverse depressive phenotypes are organized under a unified depression framework, and how substance‑use, behavior, psychotic, and neuro‑behavioral manifestations are integrated to support comprehensive clinical assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: integrating various organic‑based mood and transient disturbances that do not fit more specific categories. This structured framework aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: ضيق النَّفَس is recognized as having a relationship with مرض نفسي جسدي, indicating that episodes of shortness of breath can be associated with psychosomatic or psychophysiological disorders, where psychological factors manifest as physical respiratory symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: (“اضطراب التصرف”). This category subdivides into non‑inflammatory brain disorders (“التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)”) and psychotic/schizophrenic disorders (“الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)”). The psychotic branch overlaps with organic psychosis and includes mood‑related depressive entities (recurrent and single‑episode major depressive disorder, atypical/obesity‑related depression, mood lability, diminished affect, schizotypal mania “هوس شَبَقِيّ”). The non‑inflammatory encephalopathy/delirium cluster presents with attentional disturbances, reduced concentration, excessive daytime sleepiness, and apathy, while non‑inflammatory brain disorders more generally manifest hyperactivity, muscle hyperactivity, transient or persistent awakening disturbances, and focal brain atrophy (“ضمور المخ المحدد”), which is linked to repetitive stereotyped movements and emotional blunting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: encephalitis, psychosis and psychotic disorders, and non‑infectious delirium, and it links to unspecified childhood psychosis (ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة), manic excitement (هوس شَبَقِيّ), blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة), the various major depressive disorders (including full remission), atypical depression and mood lability. Non‑infectious encephalitis serves as a parent category for sleep‑wake disturbances—transient disorder of awakening (الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه) and continuous disorder of awakening (الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره)—as well as for motor disturbances such as hyperkinetic activity, hypermuscular activity and excessive daytime sleepiness. Apathy (اللامبالاة) is classified as a member of non‑infectious delirium. The second summary emphasizes that non‑infectious delirium (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)) is a clinical group that includes attention disorders with marked poor concentration and early‑stage

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 128
retrieved_docs.content: أو استمراره, فرط النشاط الحركي, فرط النشاط العضلي, فرط النوم في النهار الأولي, اللامبالاة, خلل في الانتباه, and ضعف التركيز, characterizing non‑infectious encephalopathy. Excessive daytime sleepiness (فرط النوم في النهار الأولي) is classified as a manifestation of non‑organic delirium/brain dysfunction (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي SMQ), whereas organic delirium (ذُهان عضوي) shows a broader relationship with هوس شَبَقِيّ, indicating that organic and non‑organic forms of delirium are linked to distinct clinical presentations yet both intersect with broader psychotic disorders. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how mental symptoms, organic and non‑organic psychotic states, attention‑deficit, conduct disorders, a wide array of depressive subtypes, childhood psychoses, and neurobehavioral/sleep abnormalities are organized within the diagnostic framework, supporting accurate assessment and targeted patient care.
